content.problemStatement:
Let's now implement dynamic programming to solve this problem.

You have a knapsack that can take upto WmaxW_{max}Wmax​ weight. 

Given NNN items, ithi^{th}ith of them having the weight WiW_iWi​ and value ViV_iVi​. You can pick some or all the items completely so that their weight does not exceed WmaxW_{max}Wmax​. 

Your task is to maximise the total value of the items you will carry in your bag.

Note that you cannot take any item in fractions; that is, you either pick it or don't.


Input Format

The first line contains a two integer NNN and WmaxW_{max}Wmax​, denoting the length of array AAA and the maximum weight you can carry.
Next NNN lines contains the description of the items.

ithi^{th}ith line contains two integers WiW_iWi​ and ViV_iVi​, denoting the Weight and Value of the ithi^{th}ith item




Output Format
Output the maximum value you can carry while not exceeding the weight limit of the knapsack.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains two integers N and Wmax — the number of items and the maximum weight the knapsack can hold. Then N lines follow, each containing two integers Wi and Vi — the weight and value of the i‑th item.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with the maximum total value that can be carried without exceeding the weight limit.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Choosing items with (weight, value) (5,6), (6,6) and (3,5) gives total weight 14 ≤ 15 and total value 17, which is optimal.
The best feasible item is the third one (weight 6, value 120). No other combination fits within weight 10 and yields a higher value.
Items 1, 2 and 4 (weights 1+2+4=7) give total value 6+10+15=31, which is the maximum possible.

solution.approach: The problem is the classic 0/1 knapsack. For each test case we maintain a one‑dimensional DP array dp[w] = maximum value achievable with total weight exactly w (or less). We iterate over items and update dp in reverse weight order to avoid reusing an item multiple times. The answer is dp[Wmax]. This runs in O(N·Wmax) time and O(Wmax) memory per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N * Wmax) per test case, O(T * N * Wmax) overall.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(Wmax) auxiliary space per test case.

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        Wmax = int(next(it))
        W = [0] * N
        V = [0] * N
        for i in range(N):
            W[i] = int(next(it))
            V[i] = int(next(it))
        dp = [0] * (Wmax + 1)
        for i in range(N):
            wi, vi = W[i], V[i]
            for w in range(Wmax, wi - 1, -1):
                cand = dp[w - wi] + vi
                if cand > dp[w]:
                    dp[w] = cand
        out_lines.append(str(dp[Wmax]))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
